hypothesis: cold `meda run ubuntu:latest --name X` + wait for SSH, carried over from autoresearch/boot-ms (same host, same image).

add `meda snapshot` + `meda restore` wrapping ch-remote snapshot / cloud-hypervisor --restore. Restore-in-place, single-VM-per-snapshot. 29× faster than cold boot. Manual profile showed ~520ms restore-only; the extra ~450ms is meda's setup_networking (iptables re-checks) and process spawn overhead on the hot path. Iter 2 candidate: skip setup_networking when the tap already exists from prior boot.

fast-path in snapshot::restore — filesystem check for /sys/class/net/<tap> skips setup_networking when the tap survived the prior stop. Small win (970→929ms, within noise). The real residual is CH's restore+resume itself (~450ms for memory+device state) plus meda's ch-remote subprocess round-trip (~20-30ms). Iter 3 candidate: talk to CH's unix socket HTTP API directly instead of shelling out to ch-remote.

CH restore with prefault=on — front-load memory page faults during restore so post-resume sshd doesn't stall on them. Effectively no change (929→924ms, within noise). Page-fault cost was NOT the SSH re-arrival bottleneck. The 400ms SSH latency after resume is something else — guest kernel re-sync, sshd listener reactivation, or conntrack re-establishment. Next lever: shrink the VM (256M vs 1024M cuts memory-ranges by 4× and should show up if memory transfer dominates).

cold-boot template with 512M RAM instead of default 1024M. Restore cost scales with memory-ranges file size (1024M → ~924ms, 512M → ~481ms, roughly linear). 256M failed to boot Ubuntu 22.04 cloud-init (OOM). bench_create.sh now defaults to 512M (BENCH_TEMPLATE_MEMORY), documents the knob. Biggest single-step win: ~2× from a config change.

user-data runcmd: `touch /etc/cloud/cloud-init.disabled`. cloud-init short-circuits on subsequent boots (including post-snapshot restore), skipping its per-boot datasource scan and module dispatch. 24% off iter-4, no image rebuild needed. An earlier attempt also masked systemd-networkd-wait-online, snapd, apt timers via systemctl mask — that aggressive set broke sshd post-restore (TCP accepted but no banner written). Lesson: mask the minimum. The pre-generated template-level asset is test_data/fast-template-user-data.yaml; apply with `meda run ... --user-data …/fast-template-user-data.yaml`.

smoltcp userspace networking wired end-to-end into restore (`--smoltcp` flag). Flow: tap with no host IP, no iptables rules — `meda netd` owns AF_PACKET on the tap, runs smoltcp with a per-VM gateway MAC/IP, exposes a TCP forward (127.0.0.1:40022 → guest:22) via a blocking-thread bridge. 46ms overhead vs kernel networking (~12% slower per-restore) but ships the architectural properties that motivated the work: zero iptables contention, per-VM identity feasible, isolation by default. Two landmines paved: (1) virtio-net offload_csum=on means the guest sends TCP/UDP/IP with empty checksum slots — smoltcp drops on RX; fix is a Device wrapper (LaxRxChecksum) that emits checksums on TX but sets Checksum::Tx per protocol so RX validation is skipped. (2) flipping offload_csum=false in the snapshot's config.json alone is not enough — guest kernel virtio-net state (captured in memory-ranges) still thinks offload is on. Kept opt-in via --smoltcp; the kernel path remains the default.
